title: Completed orders
description: Return order_id, user_id, order_date, and net_amount for all orders where status is 'completed'. Order by order_id ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT order_id, user_id, order_date, net_amount
FROM orders
WHERE status = 'completed'
ORDER BY order_id
solution_query:
SELECT order_id, user_id, order_date, net_amount
FROM orders
WHERE status = 'completed'
ORDER BY order_id;
explanation: The WHERE clause filters rows before they are returned. Only orders where the status column equals the string 'completed' pass the filter. String comparisons are case-sensitive in most databases, so the value must match exactly. The status column is included in the schema because it is used in the WHERE clause even though it does not appear in the SELECT list. ORDER BY order_id ASC produces a deterministic result.
hints: Use a WHERE clause to filter rows by column value
String comparisons are case-sensitive — match the exact value in the data

title: US users
description: Return user_id, name, and email for all users from the United States (country = 'US'). Order by user_id ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT user_id, name, email
FROM users
WHERE country = 'US'
ORDER BY user_id
solution_query:
SELECT user_id, name, email
FROM users
WHERE country = 'US'
ORDER BY user_id;
explanation: WHERE country = 'US' applies a string equality filter that keeps only rows whose country column exactly matches 'US'. The country column is included in the schema because it is the filter column, even though it does not appear in the SELECT list. ORDER BY user_id ensures a deterministic output.
hints: Use WHERE to filter on the country column
String values in WHERE clauses must be wrapped in single quotes

title: High-salary employees
description: Return employee_id, employee_name, and salary for all employees with a salary greater than 80000. Order by salary descending.
expected_query:
SELECT employee_id, employee_name, salary
FROM employees
WHERE salary > 80000
ORDER BY salary DESC
solution_query:
SELECT employee_id, employee_name, salary
FROM employees
WHERE salary > 80000
ORDER BY salary DESC;
explanation: WHERE salary > 80000 is a numeric comparison filter. Only employees whose salary value is strictly greater than 80000 are included. Numeric comparisons do not require quotes around the value. ORDER BY salary DESC places the highest earners at the top of the result.
hints: Use a comparison operator like > in the WHERE clause for numeric filters
ORDER BY salary DESC places the highest values first

title: Employees by hire date
description: Return employee_id, employee_name, and hire_date for employees with a known hire_date. Order by hire_date ascending to show the longest-tenured employees first.
expected_query:
SELECT employee_id, employee_name, hire_date
FROM employees
WHERE hire_date IS NOT NULL
ORDER BY hire_date ASC
solution_query:
SELECT employee_id, employee_name, hire_date
FROM employees
WHERE hire_date IS NOT NULL
ORDER BY hire_date ASC;
explanation: WHERE hire_date IS NOT NULL filters out rows that do not have a known hire date before sorting begins. After that filter, ORDER BY hire_date ASC sorts the remaining employees from the earliest hire date to the most recent. This keeps the result focused on employees whose tenure can be evaluated and makes the final row order deterministic.
hints: Filter out missing hire dates before sorting
Date columns sort chronologically — ASC means earliest first

title: Total order count
description: Return the total number of orders in the orders table as a single value aliased as total_orders.
expected_query:
SELECT COUNT(order_id) AS total_orders
FROM orders
solution_query:
SELECT COUNT(order_id) AS total_orders
FROM orders;
explanation: COUNT(order_id) counts all non-NULL values of order_id. Because order_id is the primary key and is never NULL, this equals the total number of rows in the table. No GROUP BY is needed since we want a single aggregate across the entire table. The AS alias gives the output column a meaningful name.
hints: Use COUNT() to count rows in a table
No GROUP BY is needed when aggregating the entire table into a single result

title: Most expensive product
description: Return the highest price among all products as a single value aliased as max_price.
expected_query:
SELECT MAX(price) AS max_price
FROM products
solution_query:
SELECT MAX(price) AS max_price
FROM products;
explanation: MAX(price) scans all rows in the products table and returns the single highest value in the price column. NULL values are automatically ignored by MAX. No GROUP BY is needed since we want a single aggregate across the entire table. The result is one row with one column.
hints: Use MAX() to find the largest value in a column

title: Fastest ticket resolution
description: Return the minimum resolution_hours from all resolved support tickets as a single value aliased as min_resolution_hours. Only include tickets where status = 'resolved'.
expected_query:
SELECT MIN(resolution_hours) AS min_resolution_hours
FROM support_tickets
WHERE status = 'resolved'
explanation: WHERE status = 'resolved' filters the table to only closed tickets before MIN runs. MIN(resolution_hours) returns the smallest resolution time among those rows — the ticket that was resolved fastest. Filtering first ensures we do not include NULL or irrelevant resolution_hours from tickets that are not yet resolved.
hints: Filter to resolved tickets first using WHERE
MIN() returns the smallest value in a column

title: Orders by status
description: Return each distinct order status and the count of orders with that status as order_count. Order by order_count descending.
expected_query:
SELECT status, COUNT(order_id) AS order_count
FROM orders
GROUP BY status
ORDER BY order_count DESC
solution_query:
SELECT status, COUNT(order_id) AS order_count
FROM orders
GROUP BY status
ORDER BY order_count DESC;
explanation: GROUP BY status groups all order rows that share the same status value into a single group. COUNT(order_id) counts the rows in each group. Every non-aggregated column in the SELECT list must appear in the GROUP BY clause — here that is status. ORDER BY order_count DESC lists the most common statuses first.
hints: GROUP BY the column you want to count per group
Use the count alias in ORDER BY to sort by frequency

title: Total spend per user
description: Return each user_id and the total net_amount they have spent across all their orders as total_spent. Order by total_spent descending.
expected_query:
SELECT user_id, SUM(net_amount) AS total_spent
FROM orders
GROUP BY user_id
ORDER BY total_spent DESC
solution_query:
SELECT user_id, SUM(net_amount) AS total_spent
FROM orders
GROUP BY user_id
ORDER BY total_spent DESC;
explanation: GROUP BY user_id groups all orders belonging to the same user. SUM(net_amount) sums the net_amount for each group, giving the total spend per user. Users with multiple orders have their amounts summed together. ORDER BY total_spent DESC puts the highest spenders at the top.
hints: GROUP BY user_id to separate each user's orders
SUM(net_amount) totals spending per group

title: Support tickets by issue type
description: Return each issue_type and the count of support tickets with that type as ticket_count, but only include issue types with more than one ticket. Order by ticket_count descending.
expected_query:
SELECT issue_type, COUNT(ticket_id) AS ticket_count
FROM support_tickets
GROUP BY issue_type
HAVING COUNT(ticket_id) > 1
ORDER BY ticket_count DESC
solution_query:
SELECT issue_type, COUNT(ticket_id) AS ticket_count
FROM support_tickets
GROUP BY issue_type
HAVING COUNT(ticket_id) > 1
ORDER BY ticket_count DESC;
explanation: GROUP BY issue_type creates one aggregate group per issue type, and COUNT(ticket_id) measures how many tickets fall into each group. HAVING COUNT(ticket_id) > 1 then filters those aggregated groups after the counts have been calculated. This is different from WHERE, which filters individual rows before aggregation happens. ORDER BY ticket_count DESC shows the larger issue categories first.
hints: HAVING filters after aggregation
Use COUNT(ticket_id) in HAVING

title: Order count per user
description: Return user_id, name, and the total number of orders each user has placed as order_count. Include users who have placed zero orders. Order by user_id ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT u.user_id, u.name, COUNT(o.order_id) AS order_count
FROM users u
LEFT JOIN orders o ON u.user_id = o.user_id
GROUP BY u.user_id, u.name
ORDER BY u.user_id
solution_query:
SELECT u.user_id, u.name, COUNT(o.order_id) AS order_count
FROM users u
LEFT JOIN orders o ON u.user_id = o.user_id
GROUP BY u.user_id, u.name
ORDER BY u.user_id;
explanation: A LEFT JOIN keeps every row from the users table (the left side) even if there is no matching row in orders. COUNT(o.order_id) counts non-NULL order_id values per group — users with no orders will have NULL for o.order_id, so COUNT returns 0 for them, not NULL. GROUP BY u.user_id, u.name is required because u.name is a non-aggregated column in the SELECT list. ORDER BY u.user_id gives a deterministic result.
hints: Use LEFT JOIN so users with no orders are still included in the result
COUNT(o.order_id) returns 0 for users with no orders because NULL values are not counted by COUNT

title: Employees with department names
description: Return employee_id, employee_name, salary, and department_name for every employee and their department. Order by employee_id ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT e.employee_id, e.employee_name, e.salary, d.department_name
FROM employees e
JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.department_id
ORDER BY e.employee_id
solution_query:
SELECT e.employee_id, e.employee_name, e.salary, d.department_name
FROM employees e
JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.department_id
ORDER BY e.employee_id;
explanation: An INNER JOIN (written as JOIN) links each employee row to its department using the shared foreign key department_id. Only employees that have a matching department_id in the departments table are returned. Table aliases (e, d) keep column references short and unambiguous when querying multiple tables. ORDER BY e.employee_id produces a deterministic result.
hints: JOIN on the column that links employees to departments
Use table aliases to keep column references clear when joining two tables

title: Products in price range
description: Return product_id, product_name, and price for all products with a price between 100 and 500 inclusive. Order by price ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT product_id, product_name, price
FROM products
WHERE price BETWEEN 100 AND 500
ORDER BY price
solution_query:
SELECT product_id, product_name, price
FROM products
WHERE price BETWEEN 100 AND 500
ORDER BY price;
explanation: BETWEEN 100 AND 500 is a range filter that is inclusive on both ends — it is equivalent to WHERE price >= 100 AND price <= 500. This syntax is more readable than writing two separate conditions. ORDER BY price ASC arranges results from cheapest to most expensive within the range.
hints: BETWEEN includes both the lower and upper bounds
BETWEEN is equivalent to >= lower AND <= upper

title: Users who signed up in 2024
description: Return user_id, name, and signup_date for all users who signed up during the year 2024. Order by signup_date ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT user_id, name, signup_date
FROM users
WHERE signup_date >= '2024-01-01'
  AND signup_date < '2025-01-01'
ORDER BY signup_date
solution_query:
SELECT user_id, name, signup_date
FROM users
WHERE signup_date >= '2024-01-01'
  AND signup_date < '2025-01-01'
ORDER BY signup_date;
explanation: A half-open date range (>= '2024-01-01' AND < '2025-01-01') captures every date within the year 2024 without depending on time components. This approach is more portable than using YEAR() or EXTRACT() functions, which have slightly different syntax across PostgreSQL, MySQL, and SQL Server. ORDER BY signup_date ASC lists users in chronological order.
hints: Use a date range with >= and < to capture an entire year
Compare signup_date to date string literals in 'YYYY-MM-DD' format

title: Users in selected countries
description: Return user_id, name, and country for users whose country is 'UK', 'IN', or 'DE'.
expected_query:
SELECT user_id, name, country
FROM users
WHERE country IN ('UK', 'IN', 'DE')
explanation: The IN operator checks whether a column matches any value in a short list of exact options. Here it keeps only users whose country is UK, IN, or DE without repeating the country column in several OR conditions. This is easier to read and maintain than writing multiple equality checks. The query returns only the requested identifying columns, which keeps the output focused on the business question.
hints: Use IN when one column can match any value from a fixed list
Put the allowed country codes inside parentheses after IN

title: Products in selected categories
description: Return product_id, product_name, and category_id for products whose category_id is 3, 8, or 14. Order by category_id ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT product_id, product_name, category_id
FROM products
WHERE category_id IN (3, 8, 14)
ORDER BY category_id ASC
solution_query:
SELECT product_id, product_name, category_id
FROM products
WHERE category_id IN (3, 8, 14)
ORDER BY category_id ASC;
explanation: This query filters products to a small set of category IDs using IN. IN is the right choice when the same column needs to match one of several exact values, because it is more compact and clearer than chaining multiple OR comparisons. ORDER BY category_id ASC then groups the matching categories into a predictable sequence for review. Only the requested product columns are selected, so the result stays aligned with the prompt.
hints: Filter category_id with one IN list instead of multiple OR checks
Order the matching rows by category_id ascending

title: Checkout-related events
description: Return event_id, user_id, and event_name for events whose event_name contains the word 'checkout'.
expected_query:
SELECT event_id, user_id, event_name
FROM events
WHERE event_name LIKE '%checkout%'
explanation: The LIKE pattern '%checkout%' finds rows where the text 'checkout' appears anywhere inside event_name. The percent sign before and after the word means characters are allowed on both sides of the match, which makes this a contains search rather than a prefix or suffix search. This is useful when business event names follow a naming convention and you want to capture a family of related actions. The query then returns the key identifying fields for each matching event row.
hints: Put % on both sides of the word when you want a contains match
Filter event_name with LIKE before selecting the event columns

title: Orders that need review
description: Return order_id, status, payment_status, and net_amount for orders where the order status is 'cancelled' or the payment_status is 'failed'.
expected_query:
SELECT order_id, status, payment_status, net_amount
FROM orders
WHERE status = 'cancelled' OR payment_status = 'failed'
explanation: OR makes the filter broader by keeping rows that satisfy either condition. In this case, an order appears in the result if it was cancelled, if its payment failed, or if both things are true for the same row. This is different from AND, which would require both conditions to be true at once. Returning both status columns helps explain why each order qualifies for the review list.
hints: Use OR when either condition should qualify the row
Both status columns belong in the result because both are part of the business rule

title: US paid users on higher tiers
description: Return user_id, name, country, and plan_tier for users who are in the US and whose plan_tier is either 'pro' or 'enterprise'.
expected_query:
SELECT user_id, name, country, plan_tier
FROM users
WHERE country = 'US'
  AND (plan_tier = 'pro' OR plan_tier = 'enterprise')
solution_query:
SELECT user_id, name, country, plan_tier
FROM users
WHERE country = 'US'
  AND (plan_tier = 'pro' OR plan_tier = 'enterprise');
explanation: This filter combines AND and OR to express a more precise business rule. The user must first be in the US, and then their plan_tier must be either pro or enterprise. Parentheses matter because they keep the two plan_tier checks together as one grouped condition. Without the parentheses, SQL operator precedence could change the result and allow rows that do not satisfy the full rule.
hints: Use AND for the country requirement and OR for the two allowed plan tiers
Wrap the OR conditions in parentheses so they stay grouped together

title: Users missing an email address
description: Return user_id, name, and country for users whose email is missing.
expected_query:
SELECT user_id, name, country
FROM users
WHERE email IS NULL
explanation: NULL represents missing or unknown data, so it cannot be tested with = NULL. The correct SQL syntax is IS NULL, which matches rows where the column truly has no value. Here email is used only in the filter while the output focuses on user identifiers and country. This is a common data-quality query for finding incomplete customer records.
hints: Use IS NULL instead of = NULL
Filter on email, but return the user columns requested in the prompt

title: Events linked to a product
description: Return event_id, user_id, event_name, and product_id for events where product_id is present.
expected_query:
SELECT event_id, user_id, event_name, product_id
FROM events
WHERE product_id IS NOT NULL
explanation: IS NOT NULL keeps only rows where the column contains a real value. In the events table, that means the event is associated with a specific product instead of leaving product_id blank. This is the correct SQL form for checking populated fields, because comparisons like <> NULL do not work the way beginners often expect. The query returns the event fields needed to inspect those product-linked actions.
hints: Use IS NOT NULL to keep rows with a populated value
Filter product_id first, then return the event details requested

title: Total payment amount for failed or refunded payments
description: Return status and the total payment amount for that status as total_amount, but only for payments where status is 'failed' or 'refunded'. Order by total_amount descending, then status ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT status, SUM(amount) AS total_amount
FROM payments
WHERE status = 'failed' OR status = 'refunded'
GROUP BY status
ORDER BY total_amount DESC, status
solution_query:
SELECT status, SUM(amount) AS total_amount
FROM payments
WHERE status = 'failed' OR status = 'refunded'
GROUP BY status
ORDER BY total_amount DESC, status;
explanation: The WHERE clause uses OR to keep only the two requested payment statuses. After that filter is applied, GROUP BY status creates one group for each remaining status value and SUM(amount) adds the payment amounts within each group. This is a good beginner example of combining a multi-value filter with a simple aggregation. ORDER BY total_amount DESC makes the grouped output deterministic and surfaces the larger total first.
hints: Use OR to keep the two allowed status values
After filtering, group by status and sum the amount column

title: Active users by country
description: Count active users (`is_active = true`) per country. Return `country` and `user_count`. Order by `user_count` descending, then `country` ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT country, COUNT(*) AS user_count
FROM users
WHERE is_active = true
GROUP BY country
ORDER BY user_count DESC, country ASC
solution_query:
SELECT country, COUNT(*) AS user_count
FROM users
WHERE is_active = true
GROUP BY country
ORDER BY user_count DESC, country ASC;
explanation: Filtering with WHERE before GROUP BY is the correct pattern for pre-aggregation filtering. `COUNT(*)` counts all rows that pass the filter per group. The dual ORDER BY (count DESC, country ASC) produces a deterministic result when two countries share the same user count.
hints: Apply the WHERE filter before the GROUP BY so only active users are counted
Use a secondary ORDER BY column (country) to break ties deterministically

title: Order amount with discount fallback
description: Return `order_id`, `user_id`, `gross_amount`, `discount_amount`, and a computed column `final_amount` = `gross_amount - COALESCE(discount_amount, 0)`. Order by `order_id` ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT order_id, user_id, gross_amount, discount_amount,
       gross_amount - COALESCE(discount_amount, 0) AS final_amount
FROM orders
ORDER BY order_id
solution_query:
SELECT order_id, user_id, gross_amount, discount_amount,
       gross_amount - COALESCE(discount_amount, 0) AS final_amount
FROM orders
ORDER BY order_id;
explanation: COALESCE(discount_amount, 0) replaces NULL values with 0 so the subtraction never produces NULL. Without COALESCE, any row where discount_amount IS NULL would yield NULL for final_amount — a silent bug that propagates through downstream aggregations. This pattern (null-safe arithmetic) is ubiquitous in financial reporting queries.
hints: Subtracting NULL from any value gives NULL — use COALESCE to provide a safe default
COALESCE returns its first non-NULL argument, so COALESCE(discount_amount, 0) returns 0 when discount is missing

title: Orders per calendar month
description: Count orders grouped by calendar month. Return `order_month` (formatted as `'YYYY-MM'` using `STRFTIME('%Y-%m', order_date)`) and `order_count`. Order by `order_month` ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT STRFTIME('%Y-%m', order_date) AS order_month, COUNT(*) AS order_count
FROM orders
GROUP BY order_month
ORDER BY order_month
solution_query:
SELECT STRFTIME('%Y-%m', order_date) AS order_month,
       COUNT(*) AS order_count
FROM orders
GROUP BY order_month
ORDER BY order_month;
explanation: STRFTIME('%Y-%m', order_date) truncates each date to its year-month string. Grouping on this string produces one row per calendar month. Sorting the 'YYYY-MM' string lexicographically gives correct chronological order because ISO format is zero-padded. This is the foundational pattern for time-series aggregations in analytics.
hints: Use STRFTIME('%Y-%m', order_date) to extract the year-month portion of each date
Group by the derived month string — 'YYYY-MM' sorts correctly as a string

title: Products launched in 2024
description: Return `product_id`, `product_name`, `brand`, and `launch_date` for all products launched in the calendar year 2024. Order by `launch_date` ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT product_id, product_name, brand, launch_date
FROM products
WHERE launch_date >= '2024-01-01' AND launch_date < '2025-01-01'
ORDER BY launch_date
solution_query:
SELECT product_id, product_name, brand, launch_date
FROM products
WHERE launch_date >= '2024-01-01' AND launch_date < '2025-01-01'
ORDER BY launch_date;
explanation: Using `>= '2024-01-01' AND < '2025-01-01'` is the portable, precise way to filter a full calendar year. It works correctly regardless of whether dates include time components, and avoids functions like YEAR() that can prevent index use in production databases. Prefer this half-open interval pattern over BETWEEN or YEAR() extraction.
hints: Filter with a half-open interval: launch_date >= '2024-01-01' AND launch_date < '2025-01-01'
Order by launch_date DESC to show the most recent products first

title: Employees in US or UK
description: Return `employee_id`, `employee_name`, `country`, and `salary` for all employees whose `country` is either `'US'` or `'UK'`. Order by `salary` descending.
expected_query:
SELECT employee_id, employee_name, country, salary
FROM employees
WHERE country IN ('US', 'UK')
ORDER BY salary DESC
solution_query:
SELECT employee_id, employee_name, country, salary
FROM employees
WHERE country IN ('US', 'UK')
ORDER BY salary DESC;
explanation: The IN operator is a cleaner alternative to multiple OR conditions when filtering against a known set of values. `country IN ('US', 'UK')` is equivalent to `country = 'US' OR country = 'UK'` but is more readable and easier to extend. Ordering by salary DESC surfaces the highest-paid employees first.
hints: Use WHERE with IN ('US', 'UK') to filter rows by country
IN is equivalent to country = 'US' OR country = 'UK' but more readable; ORDER BY makes results deterministic

title: Highest and lowest order value
description: Return a single row with two columns: `max_order` (the highest `net_amount` across all orders) and `min_order` (the lowest `net_amount`).
expected_query:
SELECT MAX(net_amount) AS max_order, MIN(net_amount) AS min_order
FROM orders
explanation: Multiple aggregate functions can be computed in a single SELECT without a GROUP BY — the entire table acts as one group. MAX and MIN scan all rows and return the extreme values. Naming output columns with AS makes results self-documenting. This pattern is foundational for summary statistics queries.
hints: MAX and MIN can be computed together in a single SELECT
No GROUP BY is needed when aggregating over the entire table

title: Users by plan tier
description: Count users grouped by `plan_tier`. Return `plan_tier` and `user_count`. Order by `user_count` descending.
expected_query:
SELECT plan_tier, COUNT(*) AS user_count
FROM users
GROUP BY plan_tier
ORDER BY user_count DESC
solution_query:
SELECT plan_tier, COUNT(*) AS user_count
FROM users
GROUP BY plan_tier
ORDER BY user_count DESC;
explanation: Grouping by a categorical column and counting is the canonical segmentation query. The result shows the distribution of users across plan tiers, which directly maps to subscription revenue breakdowns. Ordering by count descending surfaces the dominant tier first.
hints: GROUP BY plan_tier, then COUNT(*) to get users per tier
ORDER BY the count column descending to see the largest tier first

title: Orders with a non-zero discount
description: Return `order_id`, `gross_amount`, `discount_amount`, and `net_amount` for orders where `discount_amount` IS NOT NULL and `discount_amount > 0`. Order by `discount_amount` descending.
expected_query:
SELECT order_id, gross_amount, discount_amount, net_amount
FROM orders
WHERE discount_amount IS NOT NULL AND discount_amount > 0
ORDER BY discount_amount DESC
solution_query:
SELECT order_id, gross_amount, discount_amount, net_amount
FROM orders
WHERE discount_amount IS NOT NULL AND discount_amount > 0
ORDER BY discount_amount DESC;
explanation: Combining IS NOT NULL with a comparison operator (`> 0`) requires both conditions with AND. The IS NOT NULL check is needed because `NULL > 0` evaluates to NULL (not true), which would silently exclude those rows anyway — but being explicit is a best practice that makes intent clear. This combined pattern is common in any query that filters on an optional numeric field.
hints: Use WHERE to filter rows: combine IS NOT NULL with > 0 using AND
ORDER BY discount_amount DESC puts the largest discounts first; NULL > 0 evaluates to NULL — state both conditions explicitly

title: Monthly user signups with a CTE
description:
Using a CTE named `monthly_signups`, compute the number of users who signed up each month (group by `STRFTIME('%Y-%m', signup_date)`). Return `signup_month` and `user_count` from the CTE, ordered by `signup_month` ascending.

This is an introduction to CTEs (`WITH` clauses), which create named temporary result sets that can be referenced like a table.
expected_query:
WITH monthly_signups AS (
    SELECT STRFTIME('%Y-%m', signup_date) AS signup_month,
           COUNT(*) AS user_count
    FROM users
    GROUP BY signup_month
)
SELECT signup_month, user_count
FROM monthly_signups
ORDER BY signup_month
solution_query:
WITH monthly_signups AS (
    SELECT STRFTIME('%Y-%m', signup_date) AS signup_month,
           COUNT(*) AS user_count
    FROM users
    GROUP BY signup_month
)
SELECT signup_month, user_count
FROM monthly_signups
ORDER BY signup_month;
explanation: A CTE (Common Table Expression) defined with `WITH name AS (...)` creates a named temporary result set that lives only for the duration of the query. The CTE is defined first, then referenced in the main SELECT just like a table. CTEs make complex queries readable by naming intermediate steps — they are fundamental to writing maintainable SQL at any level. Here the CTE computes monthly counts, and the outer query retrieves and sorts them.
hints: Start with WITH monthly_signups AS (...) then put your GROUP BY query inside
Reference the CTE name in the outer SELECT like a table; ORDER BY signup_month ASC ensures chronological output

title: Tickets by priority and status
description: Count support tickets grouped by both `priority` and `status`. Return `priority`, `status`, and `ticket_count`. Order by `priority` ascending, then `status` ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT priority, status, COUNT(*) AS ticket_count
FROM support_tickets
GROUP BY priority, status
ORDER BY priority, status
solution_query:
SELECT priority, status, COUNT(*) AS ticket_count
FROM support_tickets
GROUP BY priority, status
ORDER BY priority, status;
explanation: Multi-column GROUP BY produces one row per unique combination of the grouped columns — here, one row per (priority, status) pair. This is the standard approach for cross-tabulating two categorical dimensions before pivoting. The result is effectively a raw pivot table in long format, which is the input for most BI tools and dashboards.
hints: List both columns in GROUP BY: GROUP BY priority, status
ORDER BY priority, status gives a predictable result; each unique combination becomes one row
